name: Learning
challenge: Learn one new word or number fact today and explain it out loud to someone at home.

stories.title: The Curious Notebook
stories.reflection: Which subject do you want to practise tomorrow: English, maths, or science?

pages.text: Each evening Layan wrote one interesting fact in neat handwriting.
She practised number facts until they felt quicker and easier.
Little steps build confidence like bricks in a strong wall.
Year 3 writers often plan one clear paragraph before they draft — small headings help thinking stay organised.
Science feels exciting when you treat each question like a tiny experiment.

stories.title: Question Power
stories.reflection: What question could you ask that starts with how or why?

pages.text: Layan wrote a curious question about light and shadows.
She used a nonfiction page to search for trustworthy information.
An important reading skill is to ask, 'Does this information make sense with what I already know?'
If a word looks new, a dictionary can show its meaning in a careful, child-friendly way.
Evidence helped her decide what was probably true.

questions.question: 10 millimetres equals 1 centimetre.
questions.explain: A centimetre is split into 10 equal tiny parts called millimetres.

questions.question: The moon shines with its own bright light like the sun.
questions.explain: The moon reflects sunlight. It does not make its own light.

questions.question: Forces can push or pull objects.
questions.explain: A force is a push or a pull that can move or stop things.
